text: Droppie makes recycling accessible and rewarding. [Entrepreneurial story](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/ondernemershalen/index.html). [Retail](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/retail/index.html). 04/09/2025 • 6 minutes reading. [Henk Hofstede](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/authors/henk-hofstede/index.html). Pick up a package and recycle your plastic, packaging, textiles, cooking oil or electronics in the meantime.

metadata.source_title: Droppie makes recycling accessible and rewarding

text: For many Dutch people, this is now a weekly routine in Droppie stores. The startup - nominated for the ABN AMRO Newcomer Retail Award 2025–2026 - proves that consumers are willing to recycle in stores, provided you make it accessible and attractive. [Droppie](https://www.godroppie.com/nl) is a one-stop-shop in major cities where consumers can easily hand in all their recycled and deposit items. From textiles to plastic and from electronics to deposit cans.

metadata.source_title: Droppie makes recycling accessible and rewarding

text: The Droppie app registers what you hand in and immediately pays out compensation via Tikkie. You can also donate the money to charities or local initiatives.

metadata.source_title: Droppie makes recycling accessible and rewarding

text: Behind the concept are two entrepreneurs who each saw the waste problem from their own experience.

metadata.source_title: Droppie makes recycling accessible and rewarding

text: Stef Traa worked for years in the plant-based meat sector and lived in South Africa for a while.

metadata.source_title: Droppie makes recycling accessible and rewarding

text: Back in Amsterdam, something struck him: the waste problem. "Everywhere people put things next to the waste container that can easily be recycled or sometimes even usable. I couldn't understand it.". > “We discovered that many people find recycling difficult. What exactly belongs where? They also find the threshold for recycling too high.”.

metadata.source_title: Droppie makes recycling accessible and rewarding

text: Stef Traa. Founder Droppie. Not long afterwards, Traa met co-founder Natascha Hermsen, innovation director at Deloitte for over 25 years.

metadata.source_title: Droppie makes recycling accessible and rewarding

text:
They start talking about electronic waste. "Electronic devices are full of precious raw materials such as gold. Natascha told me how much gold is lost due to improper recycling of electronics such as telephones. Gold has been reused for centuries, but in recent years we have been using up the stock at a rapid pace. We thought this had to change."
Removing barriers to recycling. Traa and Hermsen decide to investigate why people do not recycle and talk to hundreds of residents of large cities. Traa: "We discovered that many people find recycling difficult. What exactly belongs where?

metadata.source_title: Droppie makes recycling accessible and rewarding

text: They also find the threshold for recycling too high. If you want to recycle properly, you often have to go to several places in the city. Or to a recycling center outside the city.

metadata.source_title: Droppie makes recycling accessible and rewarding

text: Finally, there is no reward for good behavior.

metadata.source_title: Droppie makes recycling accessible and rewarding

text: With Droppie we want to remove these barriers.

metadata.source_title: Droppie makes recycling accessible and rewarding

text:
With a concept that is practical, accessible and rewarding."
From skepticism to success. In the beginning, entrepreneurs mainly encounter doubts. “Everyone in the industry said: good luck with that. Getting people to your store and also rewarding them for their waste, how are you going to do that?” Traa looks back. Yet the entrepreneurs decide to take the plunge. With the help of two private investors and support from the Municipality of Amsterdam, they opened their first location on Limburg van Stirumplein in Amsterdam a year ago.

metadata.source_title: Droppie makes recycling accessible and rewarding

text: With Droppie, Traa and Hermsen are looking for a direct connection with consumers' existing routines. “We knew that behavioral change and visitor numbers were our biggest risks. That is why we became a direct partner of all Vinted's package partners,” says Traa. "Anyone who buys through Vinted normally has to collect parcels at various points in the city. We bring all these parcel partners under one roof. From day one, we have hundreds of people in the store every day who collect their parcels and also come into direct contact with Droppie.". > “We knew that behavioral change and visitor numbers were our biggest risks.”.

metadata.source_title: Droppie makes recycling accessible and rewarding

text: Stef Traa.

metadata.source_title: Droppie makes recycling accessible and rewarding

text:
Founder Droppie
To 10 locations within 1 year. Now, almost two years after its founding, Droppie is giving skeptics a second look.

metadata.source_title: Droppie makes recycling accessible and rewarding

text: The startup opened ten locations in Amsterdam, Utrecht and The Hague within one year.

metadata.source_title: Droppie makes recycling accessible and rewarding

text:
There are now 30,000 users of the app. And every month there are 22,000 people in the first Droppie store. The startup also proves that well-collected material is worth more. “Because we collect clean flows, we make high-quality recycling possible,” Traa explains. “Routers are a great example. Normally, these types of devices end up in a large container, often damaged by other devices on top of them. By the time someone looks at them, they are unusable. Of the 500 routers we collected last month, 340 could be redeployed on a one-to-one basis.”
Contributing to the raw materials problem. According to Traa, there is also much to be gained in the field of plastic collection. "Our collected plastic is only 3 percent contaminated, while in municipalities this is 30 percent. In large cities, plastic is often processed in an inefficient manner through post-separation: consumers throw everything in with the residual waste and the municipality then sorts it out. A lot of material is lost in this way. Take Amsterdam, where the municipality only sorts out 7% of the plastic. Moreover, it may never be used for food packaging again, because it is too contaminated.".

metadata.source_title: Droppie makes recycling accessible and rewarding

text: The urgency is reinforced by European regulations.

metadata.source_title: Droppie makes recycling accessible and rewarding

text: The Packaging & Packaging Waste Regulation (PPWR) requires that at least 30 percent of packaging must consist of recycled material by 2030. "We don't have those raw materials in the Netherlands now and then we become dependent on imports. We started Droppie based on a waste problem, but now we see that it is increasingly becoming a raw material challenge," says Traa. Digitizing waste flows.

metadata.source_title: Droppie makes recycling accessible and rewarding

text: The technology behind Droppie plays a key role in the growth of the startup.

metadata.source_title: Droppie makes recycling accessible and rewarding

text: The stores have Tomra bulk machines where you can hand in 200 cans or plastic bottles at once.

metadata.source_title: Droppie makes recycling accessible and rewarding

text: Droppie also developed the Drop-Bot: an AI-controlled return bot that recognizes, weighs and digitizes materials for proper processing. The reward is immediately visible in the Droppie app with the option to keep the reward yourself or donate it to a good cause. “With this approach we digitize waste flows,” says Traa. "We will soon know at product level what is being returned. That is interesting for brands and for consumers. In the future we also want to provide feedback: you often buy this product - did you know that there is a more sustainable option?".

metadata.source_title: Droppie makes recycling accessible and rewarding

text: The first Droppie store in Amsterdam broke even within a year and in The Hague things are going even faster. Growing to 70 stores by 2027. The growth is impressive, but how does Droppie make it profitable? “We are a full-profit company and want to prove that you can use circularity in a profitable way,” Traa explains. "Our revenue model has several pillars. First, we receive compensation for collected materials. Because our flows are much cleaner, we receive a higher price. We also receive compensation from partners such as Statiegeld Nederland and Verpact. Processing Vinted packages also generates income. And we also sell products in a number of stores, for example Pieter Pot. The final pillar is the data proposition: brands gain insight into consumer behavior and material flows through Droppie.". The first results are promising.

metadata.source_title: Droppie makes recycling accessible and rewarding

text:
The first Droppie store in Amsterdam broke even within a year and in The Hague things are going even faster. By 2027, Droppie wants to set up a network of 70 stores in the Netherlands. The startup focuses on distribution in the four major cities of Amsterdam, Utrecht, The Hague and Rotterdam. The so-called post-separation municipalities where the problem is greatest. But there are also challenges, according to Traa. “Further growth requires capital, but banks ask for certainty and impact investors work with strict conditions. Many circular startups struggle with this.”
Test early, test small. Finally, does Traa have any advice for other starting entrepreneurs? "Test early, test small. We simply started with one store and immediately learned what worked and what didn't.

metadata.source_title: Droppie makes recycling accessible and rewarding

text:
Partners such as Statiegeld Nederland and Verpact also quickly joined, because they did not want to miss the boat. Only by doing, you discover where the opportunities lie and you make both consumers and partners enthusiastic. The fact that we, as an innovative player in the retail landscape, have now been nominated for the ABN AMRO Newcomer Retail Award is enormous recognition."
Read more in the retail sector. Today's store is more than just a pantry. It offers customers advice and inspiration and is the place to be in contact with customers. Due to the Ukraine crisis and significant inflation, consumer confidence has fallen sharply and this affects consumer behavior. Retailers are also faced with significant cost increases in the areas of purchasing, rent, personnel costs, Covid recovery payments and energy. Circumstances remain challenging for many retailers even after Covid. [View all articles](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/retail/index.html)
